name: Kung Op Wun Sen (Baked Prawns and Mungbean Noodles)
recipeIngredient: 2 cups Mungbean noodles, soaked and cut into short lengths
recipeInstructions:
This recipe comes from my new Thai cookbook. It's easy and quick to prepare and very tasty. It's a baked dish, which is unusual for Thai cooking. I suspect the original method might have been steaming.
Consider adding water or sherry to each bowl next time. The dish seemed a bit dry initially. Cooked in individual French onion soup bowls with lids.
1. Heat oil in a wok and stir-fry coriander root, ginger, pepper, and onion until fragrant. Remove from the wok and transfer to a mixing bowl.
2. Add noodles, sauces (Maggi sauce, salt, sugar, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, whiskey), and toss to coat the noodles well. Then add prawns and toss again.
3. Divide the noodles and prawns into four portions. Place each portion in a lidded cup and close the lids.
4. Arrange the cups on a baking tray and bake at 460°F (240°C) until the prawns are cooked through, about 10 minutes.
5. Serve hot with fresh vegetables like tomatoes and spring onions.
Serves 4.
From "The Elegant Taste of Thailand, Cha Am Cuisine" by Sisamon Kongpan and Pinyo Srisawat. SLG Books, Berkeley and Hong Kong, 1989.
ISBN 0-943389-05-4.
If coriander bunches with roots are available, use them for best flavor. If not, substitute stems. The person I shared this with doesn't like coriander; it was omitted.
Crack peppercorns slightly before adding to the mix. By light soy sauce, they mean thin soy, not lite soy.
Maggi Sauce is a popular Asian condiment, similar to a thick soy sauce found in gourmet supermarkets and Asian markets. If unavailable, use thick Chinese soy sauce instead. It's shelf-stable in the fridge.
Use thin, clear "cellophane" mungbean noodles. Thin rice noodles can be substituted if mungbean ones aren't available.
Create a garnish: Cut a scallion about an inch and a half long, remove seeds from a red chili slice, and place it at the center of the scallion. Make vertical cuts, then rotate and add more. Place in water with ice cubes; cut ends will curl up into a decorative garnish.
Add a few frilly scallions to each serving before serving for an attractive touch.
Posted by Stephen Ceideberg; July 23 1992.
